‘We are not done with the famous All-made-in-FEng-
land All-for-Treland Bantry band yet. We have re-
ceived the following letter from the band:--‘‘ Band
Room, Bantry, 21st November, 1912. Dear Sir,—L
have read with much surprise your references to the
Bantry All-for-Treland Brass Band in your issue of the
16th inst. Before closing this letter I hope to be able
to satisfy you, Mr. Editor, and your readers, that there
is no firm in Ireland at present in a position to supply
& seb of instruments for a full brass and reed band
. which have been solely manufactured on their pre-
mises in Ireland, by Irish skill and Irish workmen, and
also to show you that the committee in charge of the
band was most anxious, if at all possible, to purchase
instruments of Irish manufacture if such could be pro-
cured. _If you look up your files, you will find that on
the 20th of August in this year, I wrote you inquiring
if a set of instruments for a bi band (of Irish manu-
facture) could be purchased in Ireland. I received a
postecard.in reply from you giving me the name of a
firm in Dublin to whom you stated you had handed
my letter and informing me that they had promised to
write to me direct. You gave me no answer to the
query I putb to you, viz., Is it possible to get a set of
instruments for a brass band of Irish manufacture ?
I had some correspondence with this firm in Dublin,
in the course of which no testimonials of bands sup-
plied by them were sent to me, although it is usual
to do so, and T asked for them. They sent me a cut-
ting from the LEADER, which states that this firm is
a branch of an English house, and which gives one the
B impression that only repairs are carried out on the pre-
mises and does not specifically state whether it is pos-

sible to purchase a full brass and reed band made on

the premises in Dublin. I have also made inquiries in

other reliable sources and have been informed that

I3 there is no firm in Treland in a position to supply a full
brass band.of Irish manufacture. The committee in
charge of the band, as custodians of public money,
gave the whole matter most careful consideration and
- would have much preferred to leave the money in Ire-
i land, to a reliable Irish firm, if such a firm could be
found, but were not satisfied that it was possible to
get the order executed in Ireland by Irish skill and
Irish workmen. I hope that in justice to the com-
= ; mittee and to all concerned you will publish this letter
| in the next ‘issue of your journal.—Yours tru
P MicEAEL BREEN, Hon. Sec. to Band Committee.

We have communicated with the firm since receiv-
ing the letter from the Bantry Band, and we learn
that they undertook to execute the order for Trish made
instruments for a brass band. They received a re-
quest for a quotation, which was given in due course.
They received a letter requesting references, and
in veply gave several references. The firm not
hearing further offered to send a representative
to visit DBantry. Jut the offer was nobt ac-
cepted. Time passed, and the firm wrote asking if
they had placed the order and received a postcard an-
nouncing that the order had been placed with an
English firm. We understand that the lvish manu-
factured brass band instruments could have been ob-
tained on better terms than the Bantry Band accepted
from the firm across the water. In view of the case
we have set out we think the All-for-Treland All-made-
in-England Brass Band of Bantry deserves all the
notice and comments that we have passed upon it.

Paper, and expressed his indignation concerning it. On
the circular it is stated :—"‘ It will be necessary when
applying for tickets to furnish names of persons for
whom they are required, as the dance is strictly con-
fined to Protestants.”” The Kilkenny paper adds its
comments upon this. In the course of them it says :—
- The matter has been pretty generally commented on
i the city, and considerable indignation is felt that a
number of men, all more or less dependent on Catholic
patronage and support, should have made the profes-
sion of a certain religious faith the indispensable condi-
tion for participation in a puvely social function.” We
certainly cannot sympathise with the indignation of
our contemporar This Christian Association in Kil-
kenny is a sectarian society, and if its members get up
a dance in connection with it, it appears quite nabural
{ to us that it should be confined to their own sect. What

appears strange to us is that any Catholics should feel
] . they had a grievance. What business is it of Catholics
A that a Protestant Association gets up a dance for Pro-
testants ?  We think they are perfectly entitled to do
so. And if Catholics had more grit, backbone and self-
respect the last thiug they would think of doing would
be making a ‘grievance out of rot being allowed to a
dance got up by a Protestant Christian Association for
their own sect. It seems to us that any tame Catho-
lics who ery out because they are excluded from a Pro-
testant dance are siraply contemptible.

This month the Redemptlorvists opened a week’s
Mission in Camus Church, Rossmuck, and all the week
large crowds attended. It was a real Irish Catholic
Mission, for all sermons, lectures, ete., were delivered
in Irish, and the people were exhorted to be proud of
and adhere to their native language. So pleased were
i the Missioners_with the people and the success

Mission that the time was extended.

A legal correspondent writes:—‘An amendment

tabled by the ¢ shm’ Mr. Birrell deserves more atten-

i tion than it is getting from the Trish public. He pro-
¥ poses to add to sec. 32 of the Bill the following words,
|

ik ‘including officers removable under sec. 73 of the
Supreme Court of Judicature (Treland) Act, 1877, ete.’
The effect of these simple looking words will be to
reserve the Supreme Court (Ireland) to the Imperial
Government and to completely withdraw the Courts
ke from the control of the Irish Government. ‘The eco-
nomies that were reasonably expected from that lus:
cigus Department will either be non-existent, or if they
do accrue will not accrue to the Irish Government.
That Mr. Birrell should of himself accept this amend-
ment is sufficiently extraordinary; it is for the Irish
Party to decide if they are to be offered an empty phan-
tom or a reality for Home Rule. The Police and the
Courts are the only two Irish Departments where
serious economies are possible.””

Tt is usual to run an excursion from Bandon to Cork
on Thursdays when what is considered an important
theatrical company is in Cork. Mr. Joseph O’Mara

I and his company were in Cork last week, but the Cork,
{ Bandon and South Coast Railway refused to run an
L~ excursion train on the Thursday, which is the hal

a gentleman named Rattray. A local concert was ad-
vertised for the same evening in the Bandon Orange
Hall, and a near. relative of the stationmaster was
amongst-those announced to sing.

humorous contempt by the manhood of Ircland. A
correspondent from the West refers to them as “‘ the
truly rurals.””  We see that the poor fellows let fly a
wordy *‘ Long Tom’’ at the LeapEr. Well it is really
o hopeful sign that they have some traces of natural
resentment left in them, and we accordingly rejoice.
These fellows cut a sorry figure grovelling in the gutter
before Mr. Ranting Moore, M.P., “‘ a gentleman of your
high position and culture,” and we might reasonably
give them ‘up as gone beyond recall so far as public
spirit is concerned, They gloried in their tameness
and want of manly grit and fell flat before ‘ a gentle-
man of your high position and culture,” like Bigot
Moore, M.P. We are glad that we have at least
goaded the old slaves into some semblance of resent-
ment: we congratulate them on their glimmer of spirit
in turning on us, and we trust that they will return to
the charge.  Their literary product, however, is past
analysis and defies comprehension.  We read in a press
clipping kindly sent us by a correspondent that an
‘“order,”” whatever that means, was made in reference
to the IieapEr. Here is part of it:—

““ That this Council are highly amused by the
presumption displayed by the leading article writer
of the LizApER newspaper, clearly proving that the
gentleman in question is suffering from what is
known in America as ‘ swelled head,” and whilst so
afflicted he is inane and erratic and his conduct
clearly proves his inability to deal with most social
evils existing in rural Ireland.”

We are glad that they were amused, and certainly we
believe our readers generally were amused at the car-
toon and the verses which emphasised the Listowel
tames. So the ‘‘leading article writer’” is presump-
tious in dealing with their majesties of the Listowel
U.D.C., who crawled before Mr. Moore, M.P.. If he
suffers from “‘swelled head’’ their Protestant chaplain
has the advantage of him, for the Listowel stalwarts,
without his asking them, caused that rev. gentleman to
sufter from swelled pocket. We may be unable to deal
with ‘“ most social evils existing in rural Ireland '—
indeed one would want to be a many-sided man to do
that—but we certainly appear to have got home on the
Listowel crawlers. They should write to Bigot Moore,
M.P., requesting him to ask a question about us in the
House.

The postmaster at Enfield, Co. Meath, is about to
leave. Who will get the vacancy ? Mr. Norway hav-
ing been dumped over here, the public would do well to
keep an eye on the filling of vacancies.

ALF FOX ON THE BOLTON ELECTION.

Alf Fox sat down in deep despair
O’er Bolton’s Tory muster.

““ Oh there,”” he groaned, ** they little care
For Ulster’s bogey bluster.

John Redmond might old Carson lead,
And Craig the green be floating,

For all that Bolton people heed
When they are out for voting.”

““ The mighty timber-armed stream
That howls of war and slaughter,

Is nothing now except a theme
For laughter o’er the water.

Tt helps our caws as much along
Among the English nation

As if it were a Home Rule throng
Or Gaelic demonstration.’

T still my sorry game must play,
The game that never ceases,

And show that Asquith’s power to-day
Ts falling all to pieces.

T must let on whene’er a kick
We get at bye election

That Tory hopes are budding thick
And blooming to perfection.”

““T must let on that every caw
Makes deep and powerful reading ;

T must pretend that Bonar Taw
A genius has for leading.

T’ll have to feign that in a rut
The Coalition flounders,

And that we have no statesmen bub
The greedy Tory hounders.” ADM.W.

WEXFORD CO. COUNCIL AND TRINITY.

Ahullen, Gorey, Co. Wexford,
November 28, 1912.

Dear Mr. Eprror,—Four Univewsity scholarships
are awarded each year by the Wexford Co. Council.
lL\e_ ratepayers of the county, Catholic and Protestant,
Nationalist and Unionist, pay for them. Their sons,
of whatever religious or political views, compete for
them. In the pre-National University days it was a
burning grievance with us Catholics that higher educa-
tion was denied us. We were told that Trinity was
open to us, but we refused to enter Trinity, being con-
vinced that its Protestant atmosphere would be sub-
versive of our faith. To-day the LEADER and its corre-
spondents and poetasters and cartoonists would take
_successful non-Catholic students for these scholarships
by the neck and thrust them nolens volens into the
National, where the religious atmosphere is alien to
their doctrines. If it is good for Catholics to prosecute
their studies in a congenial religious atmosphere, surely
we should concede the same privilege to students of

Justice and reason as good a right to go to Trinity as

North Dublin Union.

Wanted 260 Chests of Tea.

The Board of Guardians of this Union invite Tenders
from competent persons for the supply and delivery at
the Workhouse of 260 Chests (more or less) of Broken
Assam Tea in Bond, at 10d. per lb., exclusive of duty,
and in lots of not less than 18 chests each, or 25 half
chests, the numbers in each lot to run consecutively;
the Contractor to advance the duty and deliver the tea
at the Workhouse as may be required.

One sample only, of not less than half a pound, in a
tin box, to accompany the tender, and each form of
tender must contain particulars of one lot only.. The
samples will be tested by a competent expert. . The
accepted tea to be bonded to the credit of the Guar-
dians, who will not hold themselves responsible for
housing, weighing, piling, examining, cording, repair-
ing chests, and transferring the tea.

The Contractor to allow the usual fare as allowed to
retail merchants.

The Guardians reserve to themselves the right to
sub-divide the contract.

Tenders, sealed with wax, forms of which can be had
from the Master’s Department, must be placed in the
tender box here on or before One o’clock p.m., on Wed-
nesday, 11th December, 1912. P

Security will be required for the due performance of
the contract.

TEA TESTER.

The Guardians will, at the same meeting, appoint an
experienced and competent person to test samples for
this tea contract, who must have no connection with the
firms tendering, and be prepared to undertake the duty
at once, give a report in detail on the order of merit of
snid samples, and to test the accepted sample chests
afterwards with the oviginals. Applicants to give refer-
ences as to competency, ete., and to state fee required,
and place application in tender box here up to One
o’clock p.m. on Wednesday, 11th December, 1912.

By Order,

JOHN O’NEILL,
Clerk of the Union.
Boardroom, North Brunswick Street,
Dublin, 21st November, 1912,

5 a lot of leeway to make up in Ireland. An
effort was made to exterminate the race, and at times
it looked as if the effort might be successful; we were
badly beaten and were put on the run; but, unlike the
Einglish, we Irish never know when we are beaten,
and we kept up a fight even whilst the beating was
going on at its severest and we were in full retreat to
America. In recent years we have ve-formed and
vallied, and now have our hands to the work of recon-
struction and are fighting to win. There will be fric-
tion during the course of our advance between those of
us who are always in mind of what the race passed
through and those, not of the race, who are amongs
us and are self-righteously alive to our shortcoming

In addition to what we as a race have gone thro
one great obstacle to the development of commerce
and industry amongst us is the comparatively vast
world of jobocracy in our midst. In recent years the
boundaries of joboeracy have extended and the end is
not yet. Jobocracy has a semi-paralysing influence
on the country. The great diffevence between jobs and
business is that in business you must make ends meet,
whilst in the matter of jobs you merely have to put
them on the rates or taxes. ‘A business or manufac-
turing concern must be efficient or else it will go down
and out; but jobocracy, regardless of efficiency, can
sponge for an unlimited time on the rates and taxe
And jobocracy, from trout catching and concertina-
playing policemen upwards, is so prevalent and so pro-
minent and so powerful in Ireland that whilst we want
a tonic to stir us up to business realities we have only
a sedative and false headline in jobocracy. Business
and industry are not a putting in of so many hours of
perfunctory labour : business is the rather high pressure
-use of nerve, brains, judgment and speculation. A first
class business man is almost the antithesis of the
-smug, golf-playing jobocrat, whose share of the world
is charged on the Consolidated I'und. A business man
must ever be on the look out: a jobocrat can afford
.even to joke about his ignorance, being sure of his
salary. Recently a school inspector, if we recollect
rightly, in a letter to the Press contended that he got
into the Service, and once in he defied anyone to add
to the qualifications necessary for his holding his posi-
‘tion : a man having to face competition and make ends
meet would feel that he had to keep on the alert every
day in the week and let no competitor out-qualify him.
Another concrete case that will illustrate the stick-in-
the-mud spirit of jobocracy as against the restless alert-
ness of the men in the responsible lines of industry and
commerce occurred last week. At the opening cere-
mony of the exhibition or mart called ‘‘Commerce,”’
our Professor of National Economics told the audience
—and he apparently thought it a good joke—-that he
was so ignorant on financial matters that if
he wanted a loan he would not know how to
o0 about getting it. That indicates the spirit
of jobocracy: once the job s secured  the
‘holder may even publicly make a cheap _joke of
‘his ignorance. And with this spirit of jdle levity there
often goes a presumption and assumption of superior
-and indeed this particular Professor of ional T
nomics, who does not know how to raise a loan, some
time back pushed himself into notoriety by writing to
the public Press on international finance! A pract
“ing lawyer or doctor has to face competition and Yvln:b

“knew nothing of anatomy ? In the world of jobocracy
